content.problemStatement:
Chef has two nephews who love integers XXX and YYY. An integer NNN is awesome if the following conditions hold:


1≤N≤10181 \leq N \leq 10^{18}1≤N≤1018
(N+Y)(N+Y)(N+Y) is divisible by XXX.
(N+X)(N+X)(N+X) is divisible by YYY.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers X and Y (1 ≤ X, Y ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^18) such that (N+Y) is divisible by X and (N+X) is divisible by Y. Any valid N may be printed.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
For each test case we apply the constructive formula.
- X=18, Y=42 → N = 18*42 - 18 - 42 = 696. 696+42=738 is divisible by 18, 696+18=714 is divisible by 42.
- X=1, Y=1 → special case, we output 1.
- X=100, Y=200 → N = 100*200 - 100 - 200 = 19700, which satisfies the two divisibility conditions.

solution.approach:
The required conditions are:
    N ≡ -Y (mod X)
    N ≡ -X (mod Y)
Because X and Y are both multiples of g = gcd(X, Y), the two congruences are always compatible. A simple constructive solution is:
* If X = 1 and Y = 1, any N works; we output 1.
* If X = 1 (Y > 1), choose N = Y‑1 because N+1 = Y is divisible by Y.
* If Y = 1 (X > 1), choose N = X‑1.
* Otherwise (X > 1 and Y > 1) the number
        N = X·Y – X – Y
  satisfies both conditions because:
        N + Y = X·Y – X = X·(Y‑1)  (multiple of X)
        N + X = X·Y – Y = Y·(X‑1)  (multiple of Y)
The value of N is at most X·Y ≤ 10^18, so it respects the bound. The algorithm runs in O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
